title: How to Make Compost Faster Naturally
excerpt: Composting is natural, but it does not have to be slow. With the right balance of materials, moisture, airflow, and pile size, you can help microbes work more efficiently and produce finished compost much sooner.
author: Permatable Team
content:
Faster Composting

Composting is a natural process, but that does not mean it has to be slow. While some compost piles take many months to break down, others can produce usable compost much faster with the right conditions.

If your pile seems to sit there without much change, the good news is that you can usually speed it up without using chemicals or special products. A few simple adjustments can help microorganisms work more efficiently and turn waste into compost more quickly.

Why Some Compost Breaks Down Faster Than Others

Compost forms when bacteria, fungi, and other decomposers break down organic materials. The faster they can work, the faster your compost matures.

They work best when they have:

  • A good mix of greens and browns
  • Enough moisture
  • Enough oxygen
  • Warm conditions
  • Smaller pieces of material to break down

If even one of these factors is off, composting slows down.

1. Chop Materials Into Smaller Pieces

One of the easiest ways to speed up compost naturally is to make your materials smaller before adding them.

Smaller scraps break down faster because they have more surface area for microbes to work on.

This is especially helpful for:

  • Vegetable peels
  • Fruit scraps
  • Cardboard
  • Dry leaves
  • Plant stems

You do not need to make everything tiny, but cutting up bulky materials can make a noticeable difference.

2. Get the Green and Brown Balance Right

A compost pile needs both nitrogen-rich greens and carbon-rich browns to decompose well.

Greens

  • Fruit and vegetable scraps
  • Coffee grounds
  • Grass clippings
  • Tea leaves
  • Fresh garden trimmings

Browns

  • Dry leaves
  • Shredded cardboard
  • Newspaper
  • Straw
  • Paper towels

A common mistake is using too many greens or too many browns. Too many greens can make the pile wet and smelly. Too many browns can make it dry and slow.

A simple guideline is to use about 2 to 3 parts browns for every 1 part greens by volume.

3. Keep the Pile Moist

Microorganisms need moisture to stay active. If the pile is too dry, decomposition slows down. If it is too wet, the pile becomes compacted and loses oxygen.

The goal is to keep compost feeling like a wrung-out sponge.

To speed things up:

  • Water dry piles lightly while turning
  • Add dry browns to wet piles
  • Protect the pile from heavy rain if needed

Moisture balance is one of the biggest factors in compost speed.

4. Turn the Pile Regularly

Turning compost adds oxygen, mixes materials, and helps heat spread more evenly. This encourages faster microbial activity.

If you want quicker compost, turn the pile every week or two. Even occasional turning helps more than leaving the pile untouched for long periods.

Turning also helps:

  • Break up clumps
  • Reduce odors
  • Prevent soggy pockets
  • Restart heating in active piles

5. Build a Larger Pile

Tiny compost piles often decompose slowly because they do not hold enough heat and moisture. A larger pile creates a better environment for fast composting.

A pile around 3 feet wide, 3 feet long, and 3 feet high is often a good size for active composting.

If your setup is very small, compost can still happen, but it may take longer.

6. Use More Nitrogen-Rich Materials Carefully

Nitrogen helps fuel microbial growth, which can increase compost speed. If your pile is mostly dry leaves or cardboard, it may need more greens.

Good natural nitrogen sources include:

  • Coffee grounds
  • Fresh grass clippings
  • Vegetable scraps
  • Plant trimmings

Add them gradually and always pair them with browns. Too much nitrogen at once can create smell and moisture problems.

7. Add Browns That Also Improve Airflow

Not all browns work the same way. Some dry materials, such as straw or coarse shredded cardboard, help create air spaces in the pile. Better airflow supports faster decomposition.

If your compost feels dense or matted, mix in:

  • Straw
  • Torn cardboard
  • Small twigs
  • Shredded paper in moderation

Air is just as important as moisture.

8. Keep the Pile Warm

Warm compost breaks down faster than cold compost. Microbial activity naturally increases in warmer conditions, especially in spring and summer.

You can help the pile stay warm by:

  • Building it large enough to hold heat
  • Placing it in a good location
  • Insulating it lightly with straw or leaves in cooler weather
  • Avoiding constant disturbance in cold temperatures

You cannot control the weather, but you can create better conditions for retaining warmth.

9. Avoid Adding Slow Materials in Large Amounts

Some materials naturally take longer to break down. These are not always bad, but they can slow the pile if added in excess.

Examples include:

  • Thick branches
  • Whole corn cobs
  • Large citrus peels
  • Avocado pits
  • Woody stems

If you use these materials, break them into smaller pieces or add them in moderation.

10. Start With a Good Batch Mix

A compost pile often heats and breaks down faster when it begins with a decent volume of mixed materials rather than being built too slowly over time.

If possible, start with a balanced mix of:

  • Greens
  • Browns
  • Moisture
  • Coarse material for airflow

A pile built in one good batch often becomes active more quickly than one fed in tiny amounts.

11. Cover Fresh Food Scraps With Browns

When greens are buried or covered with dry browns, the pile stays more balanced and less likely to lose airflow. This helps compost stay active rather than becoming soggy.

Every time you add kitchen scraps, follow with:

  • Dry leaves
  • Shredded cardboard
  • Paper
  • Straw

This simple habit supports faster, cleaner decomposition.

12. Be Consistent With Maintenance

Fast compost usually comes from regular attention. You do not need to manage it every day, but checking it now and then makes a difference.

A quick routine might include:

  • Checking moisture
  • Adding browns when needed
  • Turning the pile every week or two
  • Chopping larger items before adding them

Small, consistent actions usually work better than big corrections later.

What Not to Do if You Want Fast Compost

To avoid slowing the process:

  • Do not let the pile dry out completely
  • Do not overload it with wet scraps
  • Do not ignore airflow
  • Do not add meat, dairy, or oily foods
  • Do not expect large, woody items to break down quickly

Speed comes from balance, not from adding more waste at random.

How to Know Your Compost Is Speeding Up

You may notice a few signs when your compost becomes more active:

  • The pile warms up
  • Materials shrink more quickly
  • Scraps become less recognizable
  • The texture becomes darker and more crumbly
  • The smell stays earthy rather than sour

These are all signs that decomposition is improving.

Final Thoughts

If you want to make compost faster naturally, focus on the basics that support healthy decomposition: smaller materials, a good green-to-brown balance, proper moisture, enough airflow, and a pile large enough to stay active.

You do not need special additives or complicated methods. In most cases, faster compost comes from better habits, not extra products.

With a few simple adjustments, your compost pile can break down more quickly and produce rich compost sooner.
